html:
Orca Energy Group: Cash-Return Stub, Not a Tanzania Gas Producer

ORXGF is no longer the old “Tanzania monopoly gas supplier at 1x FCF” thesis. The April 13, 2026 transaction changed the story: Orca agreed to sell 100% of its Tanzanian operating business, including PAEM/PAET and Songo Songo, to Taifa Gas and Amber Energy for nominal consideration of $10, while retaining the right to pull cash out before closing and receive 50% of certain extraordinary income before closing. Orca says those assets are 100% of its operating business, and that after closing it will have no further exposure to the Songo Songo license extension or government arbitrations (company release).

That sounds ugly until the balance sheet is rebuilt. Orca is becoming a liquidation and capital-return vehicle. The variant perception is that investors still see a politically risky, late-filing, OTC Tanzanian E&P, when the equity is now mainly a claim on cash already generated, cash that can still be extracted before close, and the removal of liabilities that were trapping that cash.

The old bull case had one major factual problem: Songo Songo no longer deserves a long-duration multiple. The PSA/license expires in October 2026, and the 2025 reserve report valued 2P future net revenue at only $31.6 million NPV10 because reserves are limited to the remaining primary term (2025 reserves release). Orca’s board is effectively saying: the operating asset is not worth fighting for if it requires retaining cash against Tanzanian tax, litigation, license-extension and development uncertainty. That is the right decision.

The current upside comes from a simpler question: how much cash reaches shareholders?

At Q3 2025, Orca had $127.9 million of cash, $56.2 million of working capital, no remaining IFC term debt after the February 2025 repayment, and 19.765 million shares outstanding. Cash flow was boosted by TANESCO finally paying the $52.0 million settlement, of which Orca retained about $35.5 million, and TANESCO had also paid almost all of the October 2025 invoice after quarter-end (Q3 report). The cash balance was real, not just an accounting receivable.

Since then, Orca has paid two large distributions: C$1.00/share in October 2025 and C$2.00/share in February 2026, plus regular C$0.10 quarterly dividends. The February special dividend release said Orca had returned about $122.9 million to shareholders to date and still had sufficient liquidity after payment (special dividend release). Research Capital’s post-transaction estimate put residual value around $65.7 million, or C$4.55/share, before giving much credit for 2026 pre-close FCF (Energy Report summary).

Using the user-supplied current market cap of $50.8 million and 19.765 million shares, ORXGF is around $2.57/share. That is materially below a conservative cash-extraction value. A practical bridge looks like this:

Starting cash at Q3 2025: $127.9 million
Less post-Q3 special dividends: roughly $43 million
Less regular dividends since Q3: roughly $3 million
Less restricted AGS judgment security: $24.7 million
Less IFC participating interest, transaction costs and stub overhead: roughly $6 million
Add Q4 2025 and 2026 pre-close free cash that can be extracted: roughly $12 million to $25 million

That produces a rough distributable value range of $64 million to $80 million, or $3.25 to $4.05/share. If the restricted cash is partly released or transferred cleanly with PAET while Orca keeps more pre-close cash, the value can push above $4.50/share. This is not an EV/FCF illusion caused by idle cash. The cash is the asset, and management has already proved willingness to distribute it.

The near-term catalysts are unusually concrete. Orca expects to file delayed 2025 financials by May 22, 2026; the MCTO restricts CEO/CFO trading, not public trading, and the company said there is no undisclosed material information and no insolvency proceeding (MCTO update). The annual and special meeting is set for June 17, 2026. Shaymar, the major shareholder, has indicated support for the transaction. Each of those reduces the discount if completed cleanly.

The biggest risk is not commodity price. It is deal leakage. The sale needs Tanzanian approvals, TSXV acceptance, shareholder approval, and release from remaining IFC guarantees. The share purchase agreement also allows termination by any party for any reason. If Tanzania or TPDC uses approval leverage to demand concessions, or if cash cannot be distributed before closing, the equity value falls. The delayed annual filing also matters because investors need a clean year-end cash bridge.

Still, the asymmetry is attractive because the current price discounts a bad version of both worlds: it prices Orca as if it still owns Tanzanian legal risk, but not as if it owns the cash generated by exiting that risk. The social/retail setup is also clean. This is not crowded. The stock is illiquid, sparsely discussed, and mostly ignored outside stale data feeds and niche Canadian microcap channels. That matters because a special dividend or liquidation distribution can rerate the stock without requiring broad institutional sponsorship.

This is now a capital-return stub with a hard event path, not a compounding business. That caps the dream, but improves the underwriting. The right question is not “what multiple should Songo Songo get?” It is “how much cash gets out, and how quickly?”

Bear target: $1.75, down 32%. The transaction breaks, filings remain delayed, legal and tax uncertainty follows Orca rather than PAET, and the market discounts the cash by more than half.

Base target: $3.75, up 46%. The transaction closes, Orca distributes or clearly earmarks $70 million to $75 million of net cash value, and shareholders receive most of the value within 6 to 12 months.

Bull target: $5.25, up 104%. Closing is clean, 2026 pre-close cash extraction is maximized, restricted or excess legal cash is partly recovered, and the board distributes nearly all residual cash rather than letting a public-company shell consume it.
